---
title: sortingAlgorithms
author: navinBR
author-link: https://github.com/naviNBRuas
format:
  pdf: 
    documentclass: report
    colorlinks: true
  html:
    minimal: true
    code-copy: true
    link-external-icon: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    output-file: index.html
---

# Sorting Algorithms (Comparison-Based)
Author: NavinBR

A comprehensive guide to various comparison-based sorting algorithms and their time complexities.

## Table of Contents
- [Block Sort](#block-sort)
- [Bubble Sort](#bubble-sort)
- [Cocktail Shaker Sort](#cocktail-shaker-sort)
- [Comb Sort](#comb-sort)
- [CubeSort](#cube-sort)
- [Cycle Sort](#cycle-sort)
- [Exchange Sort](#exchange-sort)
- [Gnome Sort](#gnome-sort)
- [HeapSort](#heapsort)
- [In-place Merge Sort](#in-place-merge-sort)
- [IntroSort](#introsort)
- [Library Sort](#library-sort)
- [Merge Sort](#merge-sort)
- [Odd-Even Sort](#odd-even-sort)
- [Patience Sorting](#patience-sort)
- [QuickSort](#quicksort)
- [Selection Sort](#selection-sort)
- [ShellSort](#shellsort)
- [SmoothSort](#smoothsort)
- [Strand Sort](#strand-sort)
- [TimSort](#timsort)
- [Tournament Sort](#tournament-sort)
- [Tree Sort](#tree-sort)
- [Time Complexity](#time-complexity)

## Block Sort

### Introduction
Block sort is a sorting algorithm that works by grouping the elements into blocks and then sorting each block. This method helps reduce the number of comparisons and improve the performance of the sorting algorithm.

### Method
The algorithm works by dividing the input array into blocks of size M, where M is a constant. Each block is sorted using an internal sorting algorithm, such as insertion sort or quicksort. After the blocks are sorted, they are merged together to form the final sorted array.

The size of the block M affects the performance of the algorithm. If M is too small, the algorithm will have to make many comparisons, leading to a slower sorting time. If M is too large, the blocks may not be fully sorted, leading to a longer merging time. A common choice for M is around sqrt(n), where n is the number of elements in the array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of block sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the blocks are sorted randomly. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the blocks are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of block sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the blocks while they are being sorted and merged.

Block sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function blockSort(array A, int M)
int n = length of A
divide A into blocks of size M
sort each block using an internal sorting algorithm
merge the blocks together to form the final sorted array
end function

```

## Bubble Sort

### Introduction
Bubble sort is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list, compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order. The algorithm gets its name from the way smaller elements "bubble" to the top of the list.

### Method
The algorithm works by starting at the beginning of the list and comparing the first two elements. If the first element is larger than the second element, they are swapped. The algorithm then moves to the next pair of elements and repeats the process. This process is repeated for each pair of elements until the end of the list is reached.

After the first pass, the largest element will have "bubbled" to the end of the list. The algorithm then repeats the process for the remaining n-1 elements, ignoring the last element since it is guaranteed to be sorted. This process is repeated until the list is fully sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of bubble sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are randomly ordered. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of bubble sort is O(1), as the algorithm only requires a constant amount of memory to store a few variables.

Bubble sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function bubbleSort(array A)
int n = length of A
repeat
swapped = false
for i = 0 to n-2
if A[i] > A[i + 1]
swap A[i] and A[i + 1]
swapped = true
end for
n = n - 1
until not swapped
end function
```

## Cocktail Shaker Sort

### Introduction
Cocktail shaker sort, also known as bidirectional bubble sort, is a variation of bubble sort that sorts the elements in both directions. This means that after each pass, the algorithm sorts both the highest and lowest elements in the list, effectively reducing the number of passes required to sort the entire list.

### Method
The algorithm works by first checking the largest element in the list and swapping it with the last element in the list. The algorithm then moves in the opposite direction and checks the smallest element in the list, swapping it with the first element in the list. The algorithm then repeats the process for the remaining n-2 elements, ignoring the two elements that are now guaranteed to be sorted. This process is repeated until the list is fully sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of cocktail shaker sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are randomly ordered. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of cocktail shaker sort is O(1), as the algorithm only requires a constant amount of memory to store a few variables.

Cocktail shaker sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function cocktailShakerSort(array A)
int n = length of A
int left = 0
int right = n - 1
repeat
for i = left to right
if A[i] > A[i + 1]
swap A[i] and A[i + 1]
swapped = true
end for
right = right - 1
for i = right down to left
if A[i - 1] > A[i]
swap A[i - 1] and A[i]
swapped = true
end for
left = left + 1
until not swapped
end function
```

## Comb Sort

### Introduction
Comb sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that is a variation of bubble sort. The algorithm works by using a gap value to compare elements that are far apart from each other, effectively reducing the number of passes required to sort the entire list.

### Method
The algorithm works by first calculating the gap value between the elements to be compared. The gap value starts at n-1 (where n is the length of the array) and is gradually decreased by a shrink factor until it reaches 1.

In each iteration of the algorithm, the elements that are gap elements apart from each other are compared and swapped if they are out of order. The algorithm continues until the gap value reaches 1 and the list is fully sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of comb sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n^2/2^p), where p is the number of increments used to calculate the gap value. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of comb sort is O(1), as the algorithm only requires a constant amount of memory to store a few variables.

Comb sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function combSort(array A)
int n = length of A
int gap = n
bool swapped = true
repeat
gap = floor(gap / 1.3)
if gap = 0
gap = 1
end if
swapped = false
for i = 0 to n - gap - 1
if A[i] > A[i + gap]
swap A[i] and A[i + gap]
swapped = true
end if
end for
until gap = 1 and not swapped
end function
```

## Cube Sort

### Introduction
Cube sort is a sorting algorithm that uses the three-dimensional structure of the array to sort the elements in ascending or descending order. It is a comparison-based algorithm that performs better than other algorithms in some specific cases.

### Time Complexity
The time complexity of Cube Sort is O(n^3/2), where n is the number of elements to be sorted. This makes it more efficient than other algorithms in terms of time complexity.

### Method
The algorithm works by dividing the array into smaller cubes and then sorting each cube individually. After sorting each cube, the cubes are merged together to form a sorted array. The merging of the cubes is done by using a comparison-based sorting algorithm like Merge Sort or Quick Sort.

### Pseudocode
```
function cubeSort(arr)
cube_size = ceil(cuberoot(n))
for i = 0 to cube_size
sort subarray arr[i*cube_size : (i+1)cube_size-1]
for i = 1 to cube_size
merge subarray arr[(i-1)cube_size : icube_size-1] and arr[icube_size : (i+1)*cube_size-1]
return arr
```
## Cycle Sort

### Introduction
Cycle Sort is an in-place and unstable sorting algorithm that is efficient in terms of both time and space complexity. It minimizes the number of writes to the input array, making it a useful algorithm in certain scenarios where writing to memory is a bottleneck.

### Time Complexity
The time complexity of Cycle Sort is O(n^2), where n is the number of elements to be sorted. 

### Stability
Cycle Sort is an unstable sorting algorithm, meaning that the order of equal elements is not preserved in the sorted array.

### Method
The algorithm works by iterating through the array and placing each element in its correct position, reducing the number of writes to the input array. The algorithm performs a series of cycles, where in each cycle, an element is placed in its correct position.

### Pseudocode
```
function cycleSort(arr)
writes = 0
for cycle_start = 0 to n-2
item = arr[cycle_start]
pos = cycle_start
for i = cycle_start + 1 to n-1
if arr[i] < item
pos += 1
if pos == cycle_start
continue
while item == arr[pos]
pos += 1
item, arr[pos] = arr[pos], item
writes += 1
while pos != cycle_start
pos = cycle_start
for i = cycle_start + 1 to n-1
if arr[i] < item
pos += 1
while item == arr[pos]
pos += 1
item, arr[pos] = arr[pos], item
writes += 1
return arr
```

## Exchange Sort

### Introduction
Exchange sort, also known as swapping sort, is a simple sorting algorithm that repeatedly steps through the list to be sorted, compares each pair of adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order.

### Method
The algorithm works by iterating through the list, comparing each pair of adjacent elements and swapping them if they are in the wrong order. This process is repeated until the list is sorted.

### Time Complexity
The time complexity of exchange sort is O(n^2) in the worst case, as it requires n-1 comparisons for the first pass, n-2 comparisons for the second pass, and so on, until 1 comparison in the last pass. 

The memory complexity of exchange sort is O(1), as it only requires a constant amount of memory to store temporary variables for swapping elements.

Exchange sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function exchangeSort(array A)
for i = 0 to n - 2
for j = i + 1 to n - 1
if A[i] > A[j]
swap A[i] and A[j]
end if
end for
end for
end function
```

## Gnome Sort

### Introduction
Gnome sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that is similar to the insertion sort algorithm. The algorithm is named after the garden gnomes, who sort objects by repeatedly comparing and swapping elements that are out of order.

### Method
The algorithm works by iterating over the list of elements and comparing each element with its adjacent element. If the elements are out of order, they are swapped. The iteration continues until all elements are sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of gnome sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are randomly ordered. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of gnome sort is O(1), as the algorithm only requires a constant amount of memory to store a few variables.

Gnome sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function gnomeSort(array A)
int i = 1
while i < length of A
if A[i-1] <= A[i]
i = i + 1
else
swap A[i] and A[i-1]
i = i - 1
end if
end while
end function
```

## Heapsort

### Introduction
Heapsort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that can be thought of as an improved version of selection sort. The algorithm works by dividing the input into a max heap and a sorted array, and repeatedly removing the maximum element from the max heap and adding it to the sorted array.

### Method
The algorithm starts by building a max heap from the input array. The max heap is then repeatedly reduced by swapping the maximum element with the last element in the heap, and then "sifting down" the new root element to restore the max heap property.

### Time Complexity
The time complexity of heapsort is O(n log n), where n is the number of elements in the input array.

### Pseudocode
```
function heapSort(array A)
int n = length of A
buildMaxHeap(A, n)
for i = n - 1 down to 1
swap A[0] and A[i]
n = n - 1
maxHeapify(A, 0, n)
end for
end function

function buildMaxHeap(array A, int n)
for i = floor(n / 2) down to 0
maxHeapify(A, i, n)
end for
end function

function maxHeapify(array A, int i, int n)
int left = 2 * i + 1
int right = 2 * i + 2
int largest = i
if left < n and A[left] > A[i]
largest = left
end if
if right < n and A[right] > A[largest]
largest = right
end if
if largest != i
swap A[i] and A[largest]
maxHeapify(A, largest, n)
end if
end function
```

## In-Place Merge Sort

### Introduction
In-Place Merge Sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that sorts an array by dividing it into smaller sub-arrays, sorting these sub-arrays, and then merging them back together. Unlike other sorting algorithms, In-Place Merge Sort does not require any additional memory to store the sub-arrays, as it sorts the array in place using a series of swaps.

### Method
The algorithm begins by dividing the input array into sub-arrays of size 1, which are considered to be sorted. The sub-arrays are then merged back together two at a time, with the merging process taking care of sorting the elements.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of In-Place Merge Sort is O(n log n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), as the merging process takes O(n) time and is repeated log n times. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the elements are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of In-Place Merge Sort is O(1), as the algorithm does not require any additional memory to store the sub-arrays.

In-Place Merge Sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function inPlaceMergeSort(array A, int l, int r)
if l < r
int m = (l + r) / 2
inPlaceMergeSort(A, l, m)
inPlaceMergeSort(A, m + 1, r)
inPlaceMerge(A, l, m, r)
end if
end function

function inPlaceMerge(array A, int l, int m, int r)
int i = l
int j = m + 1
while i <= m and j <= r
if A[i] <= A[j]
i = i + 1
else
swap A[i] with A[j]
j = j + 1
m = m + 1
end if
end while
end function
```

## IntroSort

### Introduction
IntroSort is a hybrid sorting algorithm that uses a combination of quicksort, heapsort, and insertion sort. The algorithm starts by using quicksort, but switches to heapsort if the recursion depth exceeds a certain limit. If the array is nearly sorted, the algorithm switches to insertion sort for better performance.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into smaller sub-arrays using the quicksort method. If the recursion depth exceeds a certain limit, the algorithm switches to heapsort. If the sub-array becomes nearly sorted, the algorithm switches to insertion sort for better performance.

The algorithm continues until the entire input array is sorted.

### Time Complexity
The average-case time complexity of IntroSort is O(n log n), making it a highly efficient sorting algorithm. The worst-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the input array is nearly sorted or when the recursion depth is limited.

The memory complexity of IntroSort is O(log n), as the algorithm uses a small amount of memory to store the recursion depth and other variables.

IntroSort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function IntroSort(array A, int l, int r)
int n = r - l + 1
if n <= 16
insertionSort(A, l, r)
return
end if
int maxDepth = 2 * log2(n)
IntroSortHelper(A, l, r, maxDepth)
end function

function IntroSortHelper(array A, int l, int r, int depth)
int n = r - l + 1
if n <= 16
insertionSort(A, l, r)
return
end if
if depth = 0
heapSort(A, l, r)
return
end if
int p = partition(A, l, r)
IntroSortHelper(A, l, p - 1, depth - 1)
IntroSortHelper(A, p + 1, r, depth - 1)
end function
```

## Library Sort

### Introduction
Library sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that is a variation of merge sort. It is used to sort data in libraries because it has a very low worst-case time complexity.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into smaller subarrays and sorting each subarray individually. The sorted subarrays are then merged back together to form a single sorted array.

The algorithm uses a binary search to find the correct location for each element, which allows it to have a lower worst-case time complexity than other sorting algorithms such as quicksort.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of library sort is O(n log n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), as the binary search used to find the correct location for each element takes O(log n) time. The worst-case time complexity is O(n log^2 n), which is significantly better than the worst-case time complexity of O(n^2) for other sorting algorithms such as bubble sort.

The memory complexity of library sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the subarrays while they are being sorted and merged back together.

Library sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
```
function librarySort(array A)
divide A into subarrays
sort each subarray individually
merge the sorted subarrays back together
end function
```

## Merge Sort

### Introduction
Merge sort is a sorting algorithm that uses a divide-and-conquer approach to sort an array. The algorithm divides the input array into two halves, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves back together to form the final sorted array.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into two halves. If the array has only one element, it is considered sorted and returned. If the array has more than one element, each half is recursively sorted using the merge sort algorithm.

Once both halves are sorted, the algorithm merges the two sorted halves back together. The merging process involves comparing the first elements of both halves and selecting the smaller element to add to the final sorted array. This process is repeated until all elements from both halves have been added to the final sorted array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case and average-case time complexity of merge sort is O(n log n), where n is the number of elements in the array. The worst-case time complexity is also O(n log n).

The memory complexity of merge sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the two halves while they are being sorted and merged back together.

Merge sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function mergeSort(array A, int low, int high)
if low < high
int mid = (low + high) / 2
mergeSort(A, low, mid)
mergeSort(A, mid + 1, high)
merge(A, low, mid, high)
end if
end function

function merge(array A, int low, int mid, int high)
int n1 = mid - low + 1
int n2 = high - mid
create arrays L[0...n1-1] and R[0...n2-1]
for i = 0 to n1-1
L[i] = A[low + i]
end for
for j = 0 to n2-1
R[j] = A[mid + 1 + j]
end for
i = 0
j = 0
k = low
while i < n1 and j < n2
if L[i] <= R[j]
A[k] = L[i]
i = i + 1
else
A[k] = R[j]
j = j + 1
end if
k = k + 1
end while
while i < n1
A[k] = L[i]
i = i + 1
k = k + 1
end while
while j < n2
A[k] = R[j]
j = j + 1
k = k + 1
end while
end function
´´´

## Odd-Even Sort

### Introduction
Odd-Even sort, also known as brick sort, is a sorting algorithm that uses an exchange sort algorithm to sort an array. The algorithm works by comparing and exchanging adjacent elements that are in the wrong order, alternating between odd and even-indexed elements until the array is sorted.

### Method
The algorithm starts by comparing the first and second elements of the array and exchanging them if they are in the wrong order. The process then continues with the third and fourth elements, and so on, until all even-indexed elements have been compared and exchanged.

The process then repeats, starting with the first and second elements, but this time comparing and exchanging only the odd-indexed elements. The process continues until all odd-indexed elements have been compared and exchanged.

This process of alternating between odd and even-indexed elements is repeated until the entire array is sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of odd-even sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case and worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), as the algorithm may take a long time to sort an array that is not already sorted.

The memory complexity of odd-even sort is O(1), as the algorithm only requires a constant amount of additional memory to store temporary variables for the exchange process.

Odd-Even sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function oddEvenSort(array A)
int n = length of A
bool isSorted = false
while isSorted == false
isSorted = true
for i = 1 to n-2 by 2
if A[i] > A[i+1]
swap(A[i], A[i+1])
isSorted = false
end if
end for
for i = 0 to n-2 by 2
if A[i] > A[i+1]
swap(A[i], A[i+1])
isSorted = false
end if
end for
end while
end function
´´´

## Patience Sorting

### Introduction
Patience sorting is a solitaire card game that is used to sort a deck of cards. It can also be used as a sorting algorithm, where the deck of cards represents the input array.

### Method
The algorithm starts by creating a stack for each unique value in the input array. The first element in the input array is placed on the first stack. The next element in the input array is then compared to the top card in each stack, and if it is greater, it is placed on the next available stack. If there is no available stack, a new stack is created for the element.

This process continues until all elements in the input array have been placed on a stack. The stacks are then combined to form the final sorted array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case and average-case time complexity of patience sorting is O(n log n), as the algorithm creates a balanced binary search tree from the stacks. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the input array is already sorted in reverse order and creates a linked list of stacks.

The memory complexity of patience sorting is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the stacks.

Patience sorting is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function patienceSort(array A)
int n = length of A
create an array of stacks
for i = 0 to n-1
insert A[i] into the correct position in the stacks
end for
flatten the stacks into the final sorted array
end function
´´´

## QuickSort

### Introduction
QuickSort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that works by selecting a "pivot" element from the array and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot. The sub-arrays are then sorted recursively.

### Method
The algorithm starts by selecting a pivot element from the array. The pivot is typically chosen as the first, last, or middle element of the array, or it may be chosen randomly.

Next, all elements less than the pivot are moved to its left, and all elements greater than the pivot are moved to its right. This process is known as partitioning.

The sub-arrays on either side of the pivot are then sorted recursively using the same process, until the entire array is sorted.

### Time Complexity
The average-case time complexity of QuickSort is O(n log n), but the worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the pivot is chosen poorly, such as being either the smallest or largest element in the array.

The memory complexity of QuickSort is O(log n) in the best case, O(n) in the average case, and O(n) in the worst case, as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the recursive function calls.

QuickSort is an unstable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value may not retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function quickSort(array A, int low, int high)
if low < high
int pivotIndex = partition(A, low, high)
quickSort(A, low, pivotIndex - 1)
quickSort(A, pivotIndex + 1, high)
end if
end function

function partition(array A, int low, int high)
int pivot = A[high]
int i = low - 1
for j = low to high - 1
if A[j] <= pivot
i = i + 1
swap A[i] and A[j]
end if
end for
swap A[i + 1] and A[high]
return i + 1
end function
´´´

## Selection Sort

### Introduction
Selection Sort is a simple sorting algorithm that works by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted portion of the array and swapping it with the first unsorted element.

### Method
The algorithm divides the input array into two parts: a sorted part, which starts as an empty list, and an unsorted part, which is the entire input array.

In each iteration, the minimum element from the unsorted part of the array is found and swapped with the first unsorted element. This adds the minimum element to the sorted part and reduces the size of the unsorted part by one.

The process is repeated until all elements have been added to the sorted part and the entire array is sorted.

### Time Complexity
The best-case and average-case time complexity of Selection Sort is O(n^2), where n is the number of elements in the array. The worst-case time complexity is also O(n^2), as every element must be compared to every other element.

The memory complexity of Selection Sort is O(1), as the algorithm requires only a constant amount of additional memory to store the temporary variables used during the sorting process.

Selection Sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function selectionSort(array A)
int n = length of A
for i = 0 to n - 2
int minIndex = i
for j = i + 1 to n - 1
if A[j] < A[minIndex]
minIndex = j
end if
end for
swap A[i] and A[minIndex]
end for
end function
´´´

## ShellSort

### Introduction
ShellSort is a variation of Insertion Sort that works by sorting the elements in a partially sorted array. The algorithm uses a gap sequence to determine the steps between the elements that are compared and swapped.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into a number of sublists, with each sublist containing elements that are a certain number of positions apart from each other. The sublists are then sorted using Insertion Sort.

After the sublists have been sorted, the gap between the elements is reduced, and the sublists are sorted again. This process is repeated until the gap is equal to 1, at which point the entire array is sorted.

The choice of gap sequence affects the performance of the algorithm. A common choice for the gap sequence is the Knuth sequence, which is given by the formula (3^k - 1) / 2, where k is the number of sublists.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of ShellSort is O(n log^2 n), where n is the number of elements in the array. The average-case and worst-case time complexity is O(n^1.5), as the algorithm requires a large number of comparisons and swaps to sort the elements.

The memory complexity of ShellSort is O(1), as the algorithm requires only a constant amount of additional memory to store the temporary variables used during the sorting process.

ShellSort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function shellSort(array A)
int n = length of A
int gap = n / 2
while gap > 0
for i = gap to n - 1
int temp = A[i]
int j = i
while j >= gap and A[j - gap] > temp
A[j] = A[j - gap]
j = j - gap
end while
A[j] = temp
end for
gap = gap / 2
end while
end function
´´´

## SmoothSort

### Introduction
SmoothSort is a sorting algorithm that is based on HeapSort and works by building a series of heaps and sorting the elements within the heaps. The algorithm uses a smooth heap data structure, which is a combination of a binary heap and a Fibonacci heap.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into a number of heaps, with each heap containing a series of elements. The heaps are then sorted using HeapSort, with the largest element in each heap being swapped with the root of the heap.

After the heaps have been sorted, the algorithm builds a new heap by merging the two smallest heaps. This process is repeated until there is only one heap remaining, which will contain the final sorted array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of SmoothSort is O(n log n), where n is the number of elements in the array. The average-case and worst-case time complexity is O(n log n), as the algorithm requires a logarithmic number of comparisons and swaps to sort the elements.

The memory complexity of SmoothSort is O(1), as the algorithm requires only a constant amount of additional memory to store the temporary variables used during the sorting process.

SmoothSort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function smoothSort(array A)
int n = length of A
build a series of heaps from the elements in A
while there is more than one heap
sort the two smallest heaps using HeapSort
merge the two heaps into a single heap
end while
end function
´´´

## Strand Sort

### Introduction
Strand Sort is a sorting algorithm that works by dividing the input array into a series of strands, where each strand is a partially sorted sub-array. The algorithm then merges the strands together to form the final sorted array.

### Method
The algorithm starts by dividing the input array into a number of strands, with each strand containing a series of elements that are in non-decreasing order. The strands are then merged together to form the final sorted array.

The merging process involves comparing the first element of each strand and selecting the smallest element. This element is then added to the final sorted array, and the strand from which the element was selected is updated by removing the first element. This process is repeated until all strands have been merged together.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of Strand Sort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the strands are sorted randomly. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the strands are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of Strand Sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the strands while they are being merged together.

Strand Sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function strandSort(array A)
int n = length of A
divide A into strands
merge the strands together to form the final sorted array
end function
´´´

## TimSort

### Introduction
TimSort is a sorting algorithm that is efficient for both small and large arrays, and is used as the standard sorting algorithm in many programming languages, including Python and Java.

### Method
TimSort is a hybrid sorting algorithm that uses both comparison-based and non-comparison-based sorting techniques. It starts by dividing the input array into small sub-arrays, which are then sorted using an internal sorting algorithm, such as insertion sort. The sub-arrays are then merged together to form the final sorted array.

The algorithm uses a concept known as "galloping" to efficiently merge the sub-arrays. When merging two sub-arrays, TimSort compares the first elements of each sub-array. If the first element of the first sub-array is smaller than the first element of the second sub-array, TimSort assumes that the rest of the first sub-array will also be smaller, and "gallops" to the end of the first sub-array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of TimSort is O(n), which occurs when the input array is already sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the sub-arrays are sorted randomly. The worst-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the sub-arrays are sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of TimSort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the sub-arrays while they are being sorted and merged.

TimSort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function TimSort(array A)
int n = length of A
divide A into sub-arrays
sort each sub-array using an internal sorting algorithm
merge the sub-arrays together to form the final sorted array
end function
´´´

## Tournament Sort

### Introduction
Tournament sort is a sorting algorithm that uses a binary tree data structure to sort an input array. It is an efficient sorting algorithm that can sort large arrays quickly, and is often used in computer algorithms and programming contests.

### Method
Tournament sort starts by dividing the input array into pairs of elements. Each pair of elements is compared, and the smaller element is moved to the top of the binary tree structure. This process continues, with the smaller elements from each comparison being moved up the tree, until the entire input array is represented in the tree.

The elements in the binary tree can then be extracted in sorted order by repeatedly removing the smallest element from the top of the tree. This process continues until all elements have been removed from the tree.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of tournament sort is O(n log n), which occurs when the input array is randomly sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the elements are randomly compared. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the input array is sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of tournament sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the binary tree structure.

Tournament sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function tournamentSort(array A)
int n = length of A
build a binary tree from the input array by comparing pairs of elements
extract the elements from the tree in sorted order
end function
´´´

## Tree Sort

### Introduction
Tree sort is a sorting algorithm that uses a binary search tree data structure to sort an input array. It is an efficient sorting algorithm that can sort large arrays quickly, and is often used in computer algorithms and programming contests.

### Method
Tree sort starts by inserting each element of the input array into a binary search tree. The elements are inserted in such a way that the binary search tree property is maintained, meaning that the left child of a node is smaller than the node and the right child is larger than the node.

Once all elements have been inserted into the tree, the elements can be extracted in sorted order by performing an in-order traversal of the tree. During the in-order traversal, the elements are visited in the order in which they would appear in a sorted array.

### Time Complexity
The best-case time complexity of tree sort is O(n log n), which occurs when the input array is randomly sorted. The average-case time complexity is O(n log n), which occurs when the elements are randomly inserted into the tree. The worst-case time complexity is O(n^2), which occurs when the input array is sorted in reverse order.

The memory complexity of tree sort is O(n), as the algorithm requires additional memory to store the binary search tree structure.

Tree sort is a stable sorting algorithm, meaning that elements with the same value will retain their relative order in the final sorted array.

### Pseudocode
´´´
function treeSort(array A)
int n = length of A
build a binary search tree from the input array
perform an in-order traversal of the tree to extract the elements in sorted order
end function
´´´

## Time Complexity

The time complexity of a sorting algorithm refers to the amount of time it takes for the algorithm to sort a list of elements. Time complexity is usually expressed using big O notation, which gives an upper bound on the growth of the time required by the algorithm as the size of the input increases.

Here's a table that summarizes the time complexities of the sorting algorithms covered in this guide:

| Algorithm | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Memory | Stability| Method |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|-----------|--------|---------|--------|
| Block Sort | O(n) | O(n log n) | O(n^2) | O(n) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Bubble Sort | O(n) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Cocktail Shaker Sort | O(n) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Comb Sort | O(n log n) | O(n^2/2^p) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Cubesort | O(n log^2 n) | O(n log^2 n) | O(n log^2 n) | O(n) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Cycle Sort | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | In-Place |
| Exchange Sort | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Unstable | Comparison-based |
| Gnome Sort | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Heapsort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) | Unstable | Comparison-based |
| In-place Merge Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Introsort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log^2 n) | O(log n) | Unstable | Comparison-based |
| Library Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Merge Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Odd-even Sort | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(n^2) | O(1) | Stable | Comparison-based |
| Patience Sorting | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n^2) | O(n) | Stable | Non-comparison-based |
| QuickSort |   O(n log n)  |   O(n log n)  |    O(n^2)   |   O(log n)  |    Unstable    |  Divide and conquer |
| Selection Sort |  O(n^2)  |  O(n^2)  |  O(n^2)  |   O(1)   |    Stable    | Selection |
| ShellSort |   O(n log^2 n)  |   O(n log^2 n)  |    O(n^2)   |   O(1)   |    Unstable    |  Insertion |
| SmoothSort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) | Stable | Heap |
| Strand Sort |  O(n log n)  |  O(n log n)  |  O(n^2)  |   O(n)   |    Stable    | Merge |
| TimSort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) | Stable | Merge-Insertion |
| Tournament Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n^2) | O(n) | Stable | Tournament |
| Tree Sort | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n^2) | O(n) | Stable | Tree |

- In the best case, Bubble Sort has a time complexity of O(n), which means that it takes linear time to sort the list.
- In the average case, both Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort have a time complexity of O(n^2), which means that they take quadratic time to sort the list. Selection Sort also has a time complexity of O(n^2) in the average case.
- In the worst case, all three algorithms have a time complexity of O(n^2), which means that they take quadratic time to sort the list.
- In terms of memory, all three algorithms have a memory complexity of O(1), which means that they require a constant amount of extra memory to sort the list.
- All three algorithms are stable, meaning that they preserve the relative order of equal elements in the list.
- All three algorithms are comparison-based, meaning that they rely on comparing elements in the list to determine their relative order.
